title: GitLab CI Pipeline Duration Regression
app: GitLab webhook / API integration, Splunk Add-on for GitLab (custom)
spl:
index=devops sourcetype="gitlab:pipeline" status="success"
| eval duration_min=round(duration_sec/60,2)
| eventstats median(duration_min) as baseline_med by project_id, ref
| eval regression=if(duration_min > baseline_med * 1.5, 1, 0)
| where regression=1
| table _time, project, ref, duration_min, baseline_med, pipeline_id
| sort -_time
description: Sudden pipeline duration increases waste compute budgets and slow merges; regression detection isolates stages, runners, or dependencies that changed.
value: Sudden pipeline duration increases waste compute budgets and slow merges; regression detection isolates stages, runners, or dependencies that changed.
implementation: Ingest pipeline completion events with duration, project, ref, and stage timings if available. Establish rolling median baseline per project/branch. Alert when duration exceeds threshold multiplier or absolute cap. Drill into job-level logs for the slow stage.
visualization: Line chart (median duration trend), Table (regression events), Box plot (duration distribution by pipeline).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: GitLab webhook / API integration, Splunk Add-on for GitLab (custom).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=gitlab:pipeline`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Ingest pipeline completion events with duration, project, ref, and stage timings if available. Establish rolling median baseline per project/branch. Alert when duration exceeds threshold multiplier or absolute cap. Drill into job-level logs for the slow stage.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=devops sourcetype="gitlab:pipeline" status="success"
| eval duration_min=round(duration_sec/60,2)
| eventstats median(duration_min) as baseline_med by project_id, ref
| eval regression=if(duration_min > baseline_med * 1.5, 1, 0)
| where regression=1
| table _time, project, ref, duration_min, baseline_med, pipeline_id
| sort -_time
```

Understanding this SPL

**GitLab CI Pipeline Duration Regression** ‚Äî Sudden pipeline duration increases waste compute budgets and slow merges; regression detection isolates stages, runners, or dependencies that changed.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=gitlab:pipeline`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): GitLab webhook / API integration, Splunk Add-on for GitLab (custom). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: devops; **sourcetype**: gitlab:pipeline. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=devops, sourcetype="gitlab:pipeline". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **duration_min** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by project_id, ref** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **regression** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where regression=1` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Pipeline stage (see **GitLab CI Pipeline Duration Regression**): table _time, project, ref, duration_min, baseline_med, pipeline_id
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GitLab audit log or pipeline UI. Verify field extractions and index permissions.


Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (median duration trend), Table (regression events), Box plot (duration distribution by pipeline).
grandmaExplanation: We watch GitLab runner and pipeline signals so broken jobs, storage, or runners do not stall your teams.
knownFalsePositives: Build times vary with cache state, runner availability, or test suite expansion.
